DescribeTheMeasuresTakenByTheEntityToEns.value:
SAIL has implemented a comprehensive Safety Policy that prioritizes the well-being of its employees, contractors, and communities residing near its plants, units, and mines. Oversight of health, safety, and environmental matters is ensured through a dedicated Board Sub-Committee on Health, Safety & Environment (BSC on HSE), which regularly reviews related policies, systems, and procedures.
At the corporate level, the SAIL Safety Organization (SSO) centrally monitors and supports safety initiatives across all operational sites. Safety performance is reviewed weekly at the highest level within each plant/unit, reflecting SAIL’s commitment to continuous improvement and risk mitigation.
A structured and systematic approach to safety is adopted, involving hazard identification, risk assessment, and implementation of control measures to maintain risks at acceptable levels. Best practices from the steel industry, known as Good Safety Practices (G-SaPs), are actively identified and integrated into operations.
SAIL undertakes a wide range of initiatives to promote safe working conditions and enhance safety performance, including:
• Occupational Health & Safety (OHS) Policy
• Multi-level safety performance reviews (Board, Sub-Committee, Corporate, Plant)
• Safety Committees for collaborative oversight
• Compliance with statutory safety regulations
• Safety training programs (induction, on-the-job, refresher, and LEO workshops)
• Standard Operating and Maintenance Procedures (SOPs & SMPs)
• Safety Talks and Toolbox Talks
• Permit to Work and Protocol systems
• Implementation of high-risk safety standards (e.g., work at height, confined space, electrical and gas safety, conveyor belt, rail and road safety)
• Safe management of hazardous processes using tools like HAZOP and Bow-Tie analysis
• Regular OHS audits and inspections
• Theme-based safety drives and campaigns
• Inspection and certification of tools, tackles, and equipment
• Incident reporting, investigation, and root cause analysis
• Emergency preparedness plans and mock drills
• Use of digital platforms (web portals, mobile apps, WhatsApp groups) for real-time safety reporting
• Safety promotion and motivational programs
• Vehicle speed monitoring within plant premises for road safety
• Industrial hygiene surveys (noise, dust, lighting) and periodic medical examinations

To further strengthen its safety culture, SAIL has engaged reputed Safety Management Consultants for transformational initiatives at Bhilai, Rourkela, Bokaro, ISP Burnpur, and Durgapur Steel Plants. These efforts aim to elevate existing safety standards and embed a culture of safety excellence across the organization.

DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionTakenOrUnder.value:
SAIL has implemented a range of corrective measures to address safety-related incidents and prevent their recurrence. These include:
• Thorough investigation of all safety incidents, with key learnings shared across the organization to ensure consistent implementation of corrective actions. The effectiveness of these actions is verified during regular safety audits.
• Review and revision of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Standard Maintenance Procedures (SMPs), and Safety Manuals in the specific area or function where the incident occurred, ensuring that updated protocols reflect lessons learned.
• Use of Technology & AI
      o AI Tools for Analysis: Near Miss incidents analyzed using AI tools like ChatGPT, Meta AI, DeepSeek to prevent recurrence.
     o Digital Systems: Online systems to log unsafe conditions observed during Behavior Interventions (BI) and SEE (Safety Engagement & Encouragement) rounds.
     o Digital dashboards for incident reporting, investigation, and safety monitoring.
• Training & Awareness Programs: Regular safety training, behavioral programs, workshops and mock drills to build awareness and strengthen safety culture. Digital tools and expert-led sessions like Suraksha Samvad support continuous learning and SOP development.
• Application of the risk control hierarchy to mitigate significant health and safety risks identified during assessments. This includes:
• Field Visits: To inspect safety preparedness and implementation of safety standards. Elimination of hazards where possible
• Substitution with safer alternatives
• Engineering controls, such as the use of technology and digital tools
• Administrative controls, including safety training, supervision, visual alerts, and monitoring systems
• Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) to safeguard workers when other controls are insufficient

These measures reflect SAIL’s commitment to continuous improvement in workplace safety and proactive risk management.

DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionTakenOrUnder.value:
As part of its ongoing efforts to strengthen safety systems, SAIL is implementing a structured Contractor Safety Management Standard across its plants, guided by expert safety management consultants. Immediate corrective actions are taken whenever significant hazards or risks are identified during site inspections.

To address health and safety concerns related to value chain partners, SAIL follows a set of proactive guidelines and initiatives, including:

• Safety Awareness Programs – Conducted quarterly, these include film screenings, safety quizzes, and competitions to engage and educate stakeholders.
• Health & Safety Rating Framework – Value chain partners are assessed and rated based on their safety practices. Those with higher ratings receive preferential consideration during contract agreements.
• Visual Demonstrations of Safe Handling Procedures – Illustrated Dos and Don’ts are displayed at strategic locations within warehouses, with updates every six months to reinforce safe practices.
• Regular Health Camps – Including general health check-ups and eye testing camps to promote well-being among workers.
• Fire Safety Measures – Installation of fire extinguishers and live mock drill demonstrations are conducted to ensure preparedness and awareness.

These initiatives reflect SAIL’s commitment to fostering a culture of safety not only within its own operations but also across its extended value chain.

DescribeTheProcessesForIdentifyingKeySta.value:
Stakeholders are integral to shaping the Company’s long-term growth and strategic direction. Their engagement is critical for identifying potential risks and developing effective management responses that are aligned with the organization’s overarching business model. At SAIL, a comprehensive stakeholder engagement framework is in place, structured around a systematic cycle that includes stakeholder identification and mapping, assessment of stakeholder perspectives, prioritization of material issues, definition of relevant sustainability indicators, and the formulation of targeted action plans.
This framework is reinforced through consistent implementation and transparent communication, ensuring that stakeholder insights are embedded into both strategic planning and operational execution. By aligning stakeholder expectations with corporate objectives, the Company enhances its resilience, fosters accountability, and drives sustainable value creation.
SAIL’s stakeholder engagement process is anchored in strategic alignment, inclusivity, and responsiveness. It is governed by a structured and adaptive methodology designed to facilitate meaningful and continuous engagement. Stakeholders are identified based on their level of influence, interest, and the extent to which they affect or are affected by the Company’s operations. Key stakeholder groups include employees, contract workers, trade unions, customers, suppliers, regulatory authorities, investors, civil society organizations, academic institutions, and local communities.

ProvideTheProcessesForConsultationBetwee.value:
SAIL recognizes that stakeholders are central to its inclusive growth agenda. The Company is committed to fostering strong, collaborative relationships with a diverse group of stakeholders, including government bodies, shareholders, employees, customers, suppliers, communities, NGOs, academic institutions, consultants, competitors, and financial entities.
SAIL has also established dedicated board-level subcommittees to address economic, environmental, and social matters. These subcommittees meet regularly to deliberate on key issues, with their recommendations escalated to the board for approval. A robust follow-up mechanism ensures that the board remains informed about the actions taken.
SAIL’s Stakeholder Engagement Matrix provides a structured approach, anchored in three core principles:
• Completeness: Gaining a comprehensive understanding of stakeholder concerns and expectations, and ensuring transparent, balanced reporting.
• Materiality: Prioritizing issues that are most significant to both stakeholders and the organization across economic, environmental, and social dimensions.
• Responsiveness: Addressing stakeholder concerns in a timely, coherent, and transparent manner.

SAIL employs a variety of engagement methods to connect with both internal and external stakeholders, actively capturing their expectations. Engagement is carried out through multiple channels—ranging from daily employee interactions to formal platforms like Annual General Meetings (AGMs). Regular interactions—particularly with suppliers—help identify key focus areas and strengthen mutual trust. Tools such as the Customer Satisfaction Index and Employee Satisfaction surveys are used to quantify feedback and assess engagement effectiveness. Insights gathered through these mechanisms are integrated into the Company’s medium- and long-term strategic planning.
Customer feedback also plays a critical role in shaping product enhancements and service innovations, supporting customer retention, market expansion, and overall business growth.

DetailsOfInstancesOfEngagementWithAndAct.value:
SAIL’s plants have consistently undertaken initiatives to support the development of Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs) through the organization of Vendor Development Programs (VDPs). In addition, Special Vendor Development Programs (SVDPs) have been conducted with a focused objective of fostering an enabling ecosystem for SC/ST entrepreneurs. These programs were organized in collaboration with the National SC-ST Hub Office (NSSHO), with active participation from the Dalit Indian Chamber of Commerce & Industry (DICCI) and the Tribal Indian Chamber of Commerce & Industry (TICCI). During these sessions, participants were briefed on SAIL’s procurement processes, vendor registration procedures, and the specific provisions available for SC/ST and women-owned enterprises.

These engagements also serve to inform vendors about SAIL’s procurement requirements, items earmarked for SC/ST and women vendors, and the benefits extended to MSEs from these communities. SAIL has also actively participated in various conclaves organized by National SC ST Hub Office (NSSHO) to further promote entrepreneurship among SC/ST groups.

To enhance financial awareness, representatives from Invoice Mart are invited to select SVDPs to educate participants on the advantages of bill discounting through the Trade Receivables Discounting System (TReDS). In FY 2024–25, SAIL successfully conducted 56 VDPs and 27 SVDPs across its plants.

Furthermore, SAIL plants provide ongoing support to local MSEs through mentoring, training, technical assistance, and capacity-building initiatives. To stimulate regional industrial development, SAIL also offers locally based MSMEs—particularly those situated in the same districts as its five Integrated Steel Plants (ISPs)—access to steel at competitive and affordable rates, supported by favorable commercial terms.

DescribeTheInternalMechanismsInPlaceToRe.value: SAIL has put in place robust internal systems to effectively address human rights-related grievances. These include specialized grievance redressal cells and committees operating at multiple organizational levels, allowing employees to report issues confidentially. The company regularly conducts training and awareness sessions to inform staff about their rights and the procedures available for raising concerns. Confidentiality and protection from retaliation are key principles of the grievance process. This well-structured framework ensures that all human rights issues are handled fairly, transparently, and in alignment with SAIL’s commitment to ethical conduct and employee well-being.

MechanismsToPreventAdverseConsequencesTo.value: To prevent adverse consequences to the complainant in cases of discrimination and harassment, SAIL has implemented several robust mechanisms. We ensure confidentiality of the complainant’s identity and case details to protect their privacy and prevent retaliation. We provide support services, including counseling and legal advice, to help complainants cope with the situation. All complaints are investigated fairly, thoroughly, and promptly. Regular training sessions and awareness programs are conducted to foster a respectful workplace culture and educate employees about their rights and responsibilities. Whistleblower protection mechanisms are in place to ensure safe reporting without fear of reprisal. Through these measures, SAIL strives to maintain a safe, respectful, and supportive work environment for all employees.   The mechanism is well laid in the Policy. For more details, refer to the Policy link (https://sail.co.in/sites/default/files/ upload/2023-06/Policy-for-Prohibition-Prevention-Redressal-of-Sexual-Harassment-of-Women-at-Workplace.pdf).

DetailsOfCoverageAndImplementationIfForZ.value:
SAIL is taking significant strides toward achieving the ambitious goal of Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) at all its ISPs. By prioritizing the treatment and recycling of all wastewater within the plant boundaries, SAIL is reinforcing its commitment to environmental stewardship. Key achievements and ongoing efforts include:
• Treatment and recirculation of approx. 128 million m³/year of wastewater across the ISPs.
• Effluent treatment facilities with a total capacity of 500 m³/hour installed at Durgapur Steel Plant during FY 2024-25.
• Storage capacity of the lagoon at Rourkela Steel Plant enhanced by 3 lakh m³ through de-silting.
• All upcoming units within SAIL plants are envisioned to operate on the ZLD concept.

To drive continuous improvement, SAIL is implementing several initiatives, including the adoption of advanced technologies, special measures to maximize reuse of treated effluents, and third-party assessments to identify further opportunities for optimization.

DetailsOfWasteManagementPracticesAdopted.value:
SAIL’s integrated strategy combines innovation, compliance, and resource efficiency to manage industrial and hazardous waste responsibly, reduce reliance on harmful substances and drive progress toward a circular economy and sustainable industrial future. SAIL is committed in its Corporate Environment Policy to minimize source-wise solid waste generation and maximise its utilisation to the tune of 100%. The 4Rs principle — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle and Recover — is widely applied at SAIL plants, enabling significant contributions toward sustainability
SAIL generates various by-products such as blast furnace (BF) slag, basic oxygen furnace (BOF) slag, mill scale, flue dust and refractory waste, etc. These by-products are not treated as waste but are transformed into valuable resources through innovative processes. For instance, molten BF is fully granulated for utilization in the cement industry. On the other hand, BOF slag is used mainly internally through recycling it in BF & steel melting shop as partial replacement of limestone and in sinter making as base-mix. Solid wastes like mill scale and lime/dolo fines are recycled entirely through sinter making process and waste refractory bricks are fully utilized through either selling to external agencies or reusing internally. 
Recognizing the untapped potential of BOF slag, SAIL is investing heavily in R&D to develop advanced waste management techniques. Collaborative initiatives with renowned research institutes have paved the way for innovative applications, such as:

• Eco-Friendly Fertilizer Development: In partnership with ICAR-Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI) under the Ministry of Steel, SAIL is exploring usage of BOF slag in developing cost-effective fertilizers. Slag Based Value Added Products (SSBVAPs) have been developed by IARI and these SSBVAPs are undergoing field trials at all the five ISPs of SAIL, demonstrating their potential for sustainable agriculture and soil improvement

• Paver Blocks Manufacturing: Rourkela Steel Plant launched ‘Eco Brick’, an ecofriendly paver block manufacturing facility for utilizing BOF slag, to promote sustainability and waste utilization. The facility is equipped to produce high quality paver blocks with a concrete grade of M200 and a block thickness of 80 mm with a daily capacity of 1,000 blocks/day.

Durgapur Steel Plant has set up a unit for manufacture of paver blocks by using weathered BOF slag (0-5mm size) in place of river sand. As a measure for increasing solid waste utilization, Salem Steel Plant has also started making paver blocks by utilizing its EAF slag through outsourced agency.
This initiative significantly enhances slag utilization, reducing waste disposal challenges and supporting a circular economy in steel production.

SAIL’s operations adhere to the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016 and it is ensured that all hazardous waste is carefully inventoried, quantified, and either safely disposed of in captive secured landfills facility or through the authorized agencies. Wherever it is feasible, hazardous materials are reused/co-processed, thereby promoting circularity and reducing dependency on virgin resources.

E-wastes and Battery waste are being disposed through authorized recyclers. Negligible quantity of construction & demolition waste is generated at some of the plants, which is internally utilized. During demolition/dismantling of specifically old structure/building, significant quantity of waste is generated which is disposed of through external agencies.

The Company also adheres to the EPR in plastic waste management. All SAIL Plants are registered with the CPCB under the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016.

DescribeTheMechanismsToReceiveAndRedress.value:
SAIL has a diverse and multi-dimensional approach to get feedback from the communities via holding regular community meetings, periodic interaction with municipalities and Town administrative committees and active engagement with the local society so as to understand if they have any views, issues, complaints and grievances related to the interventions. 

Centralised Public Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (CPGRAMS) is an online platform available to the citizens 24x7 to lodge their grievances to the public authorities on any subject related to service delivery. CPGRAMS is also accessible to the citizens through standalone mobile application downloadable through Google Play store and mobile application integrated with UMANG.
  
The status of the grievance filed in CPGRAMS can be tracked with the unique registration ID provided at the time of registration of the complainant. CPGRAMS also provides appeal facility to the citizens if they are not satisfied with the resolution by the Grievance Officer. After closure of grievance if the complainant is not satisfied with the resolution, he/ she can provide feedback. If the rating is ‘Poor’ the option to file an appeal is enabled. The status of the Appeal can also be tracked by the petitioner with the grievance registration number. Issues which are not taken up for redress:
• RTI Matters
• Court related/Subjudice matters
• Religious matters
• Suggestions
• Grievances of Government employees concerning their service matters including disciplinary proceedings etc.

DescribeTheMechanismsInPlaceToReceiveAnd.value:
Consumer Complaints:
SAIL has established a robust mechanism to effectively handle customer grievances. Customers can file a Quality Complaint (QC), after which the concerned Branch Executive inspects the reported material. If needed, support from a regional Application Engineer (AE) or the relevant Plant is provided to assist and speed up the resolution process. If the complaint is found to be valid, a return order is processed, followed by a refund, reinforcing customer trust and satisfaction.

Customer Feedback:
To enhance customer interaction and accessibility, SAIL has introduced an AI-powered chatbot named ‘SAIL SARATHI’ that simplifies navigation and provides information to users. Additionally, SAIL operates a verified WhatsApp Business account, which is integrated with the SAIL SARATHI app for seamless communication.

To further strengthen customer engagement, the Central Marketing Organization (CMO) has launched a dedicated app called “SAIL Grahak Sampark.” This app helps CMO executives efficiently plan, manage, and optimize customer meetings.

StepsTakenToInformAndEducateConsumersAbo.value:
Steel is a widely used material across various sectors. In line with the National Steel Policy's objective to enhance per capita steel consumption in India, SAIL organized around 400 "Gaon Ki Ore" workshops across the Country during the year. These workshops aimed to raise awareness and encourage the use of steel in rural and semi-urban areas.
In addition to these outreach efforts, SAIL actively participated in trade fairs and exhibitions related to the steel industry to further promote its products and applications.
To make product information more accessible, SAIL has provided a dedicated webpage where customers, end users, and the general public can explore the applications of specific steel products. The webpage can be accessed at: https://www.sail.co.in/en/application/bars-and-rods

DetailsOfProductInformationOnTheProductO.value:
Yes, SAIL has implemented advanced product identification and traceability measures for items like TMT bars by introducing QR-coded stud welded tags on each bundle. These tags provide detailed product information and, in certain plants, link directly to chemical and physical properties via a mobile app. Similarly, products such as PM Plates carry data on size, grade, and technical delivery conditions directly on the product.

On November 4, 2023, SAIL successfully launched the "Made in India" branding and labeling initiative. In addition to standard labeling, a QR code is now affixed to products, which, when scanned, verifies the item’s authenticity through a secure server managed by the Quality Council of India. This system confirms key product details—such as manufacturing plant, size, grade, and heat number—offering enhanced assurance to customers and stakeholders.

RationaleForIdentifyingTheRiskOpportunit.value: Water is a vital resource in steel manufacturing. Efficient water use not only ensures regulatory compliance but also reduces operational costs and enhances long-term sustainability.
RationaleForIdentifyingTheRiskOpportunit.rawValue: Water is a vital resource in steel manufacturing. Efficient water use not only ensures regulatory compliance but also reduces operational costs and enhances long-term sustainability.

InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrMitigate.value: SAIL is actively pursuing “Zero Liquid Discharge” (ZLD) across all integrated steel plants. This involves deploying advanced effluent treatment technologies and water recycling systems. These efforts reduce freshwater dependency, lower effluent discharge costs, and improve environmental performance, which can attract sustainability-linked financing and improve investor confidence.

RationaleForIdentifyingTheRiskOpportunit.rawValue: Industrial waste, if managed effectively, can be transformed into valuable resources. Recycling slag, dust, and plastics reduces environmental impact and creates economic value

InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrMitigate.value: SAIL has implemented systems to segregate, reuse, and monetize waste. BF Slag is used in cement manufacturing, dust is recycled in sinter plants, and plastics are handled through authorized recyclers. These practices reduce landfill costs, generate revenue from byproduct sales, and contribute to circular economy goals, enhancing SAIL’s ESG profile.

RationaleForIdentifyingTheRiskOpportunit.value: Reducing emissions aligns with global climate goals and enhances SAIL’s competitiveness in green steel markets. It also opens doors to carbon credits, green bonds, and sustainability-linked incentives.
RationaleForIdentifyingTheRiskOpportunit.rawValue: Reducing emissions aligns with global climate goals and enhances SAIL’s competitiveness in green steel markets. It also opens doors to carbon credits, green bonds, and sustainability-linked incentives.

InCaseOfRiskApproachToAdaptOrMitigate.value: SAIL is actively advancing its decarbonization agenda across integrated steel plants through key technological interventions such as CDQ, TRT, CDI, and Waste Heat Recovery systems to enhance energy efficiency and reduce fossil fuel dependence. The deployment of walking beam reheating furnaces is optimizing rolling mill operations. On the renewable energy front, SAIL aims to scale its capacity to 384 MW by FY 2028–29, transition to LED lighting, and pilot green hydrogen in steelmaking. R&D efforts are focused on CCUS, low-carbon technologies in collaboration with IITs, and slag utilization to curb indirect emissions. These initiatives are guided by a phased decarbonization roadmap, participation in the PAT scheme, and alignment with the National Green Hydrogen Mission—underscoring SAIL’s commitment to sustainable steel production and national climate objectives.

SpecificCommitmentsGoalsAndTargetsSetByT.value:
• SAIL is committed to significantly reducing particulate matter (PM) emissions from its integrated steel plants as part of its broader environmental sustainability objectives. SAIL aims to lower specific PM emissions from 0.81 kg/tcs in 2015–16 to 0.56 kg/tcs by 2025–26, aligning with global best practices and national environmental standards.
• SAIL is committed to enhancing sustainability, cost efficiency, and regulatory compliance by reducing specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. SAIL aims to lower water usage from 3.83 m³/tcs in 2015–16 to 2.85 m³/tcs by 2025–26 through strategic interventions and best practices.
• SAIL is committed to reducing CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants as part of its sustainability strategy and alignment with India’s climate goals. SAIL aims to lower specific CO2 emissions from 2.60 T/tcs in 2015–16 to below 2.302 T/tcs by 2030, with an interim target of 2.41 T/tcs by 2025–26. SAIL is also working toward achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, in line with national commitments.
• SAIL is focused on reducing Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in its integrated steel plants to enhance operational efficiency, lower production costs, and meet sustainability objectives. SAIL targets a 9% reduction from the 2015–16 baseline of 6.51 Gcal/tcs, aiming to reach 6.08 Gcal/tcs by 2025–26.
• Strengthen enterprise-wide cybersecurity posture through procurement of advanced tools for enhanced incident detection and response capabilities.

• SAIL is committed to enhancing sustainability, cost efficiency, and regulatory compliance by reducing specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. SAIL aims to lower water usage from 3.83 m³/tcs in 2015–16 to 2.85 m³/tcs by 2025–26 through strategic interventions and best practices.

SAIL aims to lower specific CO2 emissions from 2.60 T/tcs in 2015–16 to below 2.302 T/tcs by 2030, with an interim target of 2.41 T/tcs by 2025–26. SAIL is also working toward achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, in line with national commitments.
• SAIL is focused on reducing Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in its integrated steel plants to enhance operational efficiency, lower production costs, and meet sustainability objectives. SAIL targets a 9% reduction from the 2015–16 baseline of 6.51 Gcal/tcs, aiming to reach 6.08 Gcal/tcs by 2025–26.
• Strengthen enterprise-wide cybersecurity posture through procurement of advanced tools for enhanced incident detection and response capabilities.

PerformanceOfTheEntityAgainstTheSpecific.value:
Details of performance of the entity against the specific commitments, goals and targets for 24-25 is given under the Principle 6.
• Action plan for PM reduction: SAIL is implementing a comprehensive strategy to reduce particulate matter (PM) emissions across its integrated steel plants. This includes upgrading pollution control systems by installing advanced electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), bag filters, wet scrubbers, and dry fog systems. Process optimization measures such as Top Pressure Recovery Turbines (TRT), Coal Dust Injection (CDI), and Coke Dry Quenching (CDQ) are being adopted to minimize emissions. Infrastructure modernization involves commissioning taller coke oven batteries with CDQ and waste heat recovery, along with cleaner reheating furnaces. Material handling is being improved by enclosing conveyors and automating operations to reduce dust. Real-time monitoring is ensured through Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) integrated with a centralized MIS for compliance and audits. SAIL is also focusing on 100% utilization of blast furnace and BOF slag in construction applications to reduce dust from stockpiles. Green belt development is being expanded with native species to enhance dust capture and biodiversity. Employee engagement is promoted through regular environmental training and sustainability initiatives. Additionally, SAIL has partnered with BHP to explore innovative, low-emission steelmaking technologies using hydrogen and biochar, supporting long-term decarbonization and PM reduction goals.
• Action plan for reduction water consumption: SAIL is advancing a robust water conservation strategy to reduce specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. The approach centers on maximizing water recycling through closed-loop systems, reverse osmosis, and membrane bioreactors, along with cascading water use across processes. Traditional cooling systems are being replaced with evaporative towers and air-cooled exchangers, while dry quenching and high-efficiency descaling are cutting water use at the source. Smart sensors, flow meters, and regular audits ensure real-time monitoring and rapid leak detection. Rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge structures are being scaled across facilities. Employee awareness programs promote conservation culture, while R&D collaborations with IITs and CSIR are piloting low-water steelmaking and alternative cooling technologies. These efforts support SAIL’s broader goals of sustainability, cost efficiency, and zero-liquid discharge.
• Action plan for reducing CO2 emission: SAIL is pursuing an aggressive decarbonization strategy to reduce CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants. Key technological upgrades include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at improving energy efficiency and cutting fossil fuel use. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being deployed to optimize rolling mill operations. On the renewable front, SAIL is expanding RE to 384 MW by 2028–29, transitioning to LED lighting, and piloting green hydrogen for steelmaking. R&D efforts focus on Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS), low-carbon technologies in collaboration with IITs, and slag utilization to reduce indirect emissions. These initiatives are backed by a phased decarbonization roadmap, active participation in the PAT scheme, and alignment with the National Green Hydrogen Mission—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to sustainable steel production and India’s climate goals.
• Action plan for reducing energy consumption: SAIL is actively working to reduce SEC through a multi-pronged strategy focused on technology, optimization, and innovation. Key process improvements include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at minimizing energy loss and maximizing reuse. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being adopted for their superior efficiency in rolling mills. Energy recycling is enhanced through gas recovery systems and real-time Energy Management Systems (EMS), while load balancing helps reduce peak demand. SAIL is also integrating renewable energy, targeting 384 MW of capacity by 2028–29, and deploying LED lighting with smart controls to cut auxiliary consumption. R&D collaborations with NEDO, IITs, and CSIR are driving low-energy steelmaking innovations, including process integration modeling. These efforts are supported by policy initiatives such as participation in the PAT scheme, alignment with the NMEEE, and regular corporate energy audits—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.
• To enhance its security posture, SAIL is in the process of centralizing its cybersecurity operations to ensure more effective management and defense against emerging threats.

SpecificCommitmentsGoalsAndTargetsSetByT.value:
• SAIL is committed to significantly reducing particulate matter (PM) emissions from its integrated steel plants as part of its broader environmental sustainability objectives. SAIL aims to lower specific PM emissions from 0.81 kg/tcs in 2015–16 to 0.56 kg/tcs by 2025–26, aligning with global best practices and national environmental standards.
• SAIL is committed to enhancing sustainability, cost efficiency, and regulatory compliance by reducing specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. SAIL aims to lower water usage from 3.83 m³/tcs in 2015–16 to 2.85 m³/tcs by 2025–26 through strategic interventions and best practices.
• SAIL is committed to reducing CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants as part of its sustainability strategy and alignment with India’s climate goals. SAIL aims to lower specific CO2 emissions from 2.60 T/tcs in 2015–16 to below 2.302 T/tcs by 2030, with an interim target of 2.41 T/tcs by 2025–26. SAIL is also working toward achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, in line with national commitments.
• SAIL is focused on reducing Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in its integrated steel plants to enhance operational efficiency, lower production costs, and meet sustainability objectives. SAIL targets a 9% reduction from the 2015–16 baseline of 6.51 Gcal/tcs, aiming to reach 6.08 Gcal/tcs by 2025–26.
• Strengthen enterprise-wide cybersecurity posture through procurement of advanced tools for enhanced incident detection and response capabilities.

SAIL aims to lower specific PM emissions from 0.81 kg/tcs in 2015–16 to 0.56 kg/tcs by 2025–26, aligning with global best practices and national environmental standards.
• SAIL is committed to enhancing sustainability, cost efficiency, and regulatory compliance by reducing specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. SAIL aims to lower water usage from 3.83 m³/tcs in 2015–16 to 2.85 m³/tcs by 2025–26 through strategic interventions and best practices.
• SAIL is committed to reducing CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants as part of its sustainability strategy and alignment with India’s climate goals. SAIL aims to lower specific CO2 emissions from 2.60 T/tcs in 2015–16 to below 2.302 T/tcs by 2030, with an interim target of 2.41 T/tcs by 2025–26. SAIL is also working toward achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, in line with national commitments.
• SAIL is focused on reducing Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in its integrated steel plants to enhance operational efficiency, lower production costs, and meet sustainability objectives. SAIL targets a 9% reduction from the 2015–16 baseline of 6.51 Gcal/tcs, aiming to reach 6.08 Gcal/tcs by 2025–26.
• Strengthen enterprise-wide cybersecurity posture through procurement of advanced tools for enhanced incident detection and response capabilities.

PerformanceOfTheEntityAgainstTheSpecific.value:
Details of performance of the entity against the specific commitments, goals and targets for 24-25 is given under the Principle 6.
• Action plan for PM reduction: SAIL is implementing a comprehensive strategy to reduce particulate matter (PM) emissions across its integrated steel plants. This includes upgrading pollution control systems by installing advanced electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), bag filters, wet scrubbers, and dry fog systems. Process optimization measures such as Top Pressure Recovery Turbines (TRT), Coal Dust Injection (CDI), and Coke Dry Quenching (CDQ) are being adopted to minimize emissions. Infrastructure modernization involves commissioning taller coke oven batteries with CDQ and waste heat recovery, along with cleaner reheating furnaces. Material handling is being improved by enclosing conveyors and automating operations to reduce dust. Real-time monitoring is ensured through Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) integrated with a centralized MIS for compliance and audits. SAIL is also focusing on 100% utilization of blast furnace and BOF slag in construction applications to reduce dust from stockpiles. Green belt development is being expanded with native species to enhance dust capture and biodiversity. Employee engagement is promoted through regular environmental training and sustainability initiatives. Additionally, SAIL has partnered with BHP to explore innovative, low-emission steelmaking technologies using hydrogen and biochar, supporting long-term decarbonization and PM reduction goals.
• Action plan for reduction water consumption: SAIL is advancing a robust water conservation strategy to reduce specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. The approach centers on maximizing water recycling through closed-loop systems, reverse osmosis, and membrane bioreactors, along with cascading water use across processes. Traditional cooling systems are being replaced with evaporative towers and air-cooled exchangers, while dry quenching and high-efficiency descaling are cutting water use at the source. Smart sensors, flow meters, and regular audits ensure real-time monitoring and rapid leak detection. Rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge structures are being scaled across facilities. Employee awareness programs promote conservation culture, while R&D collaborations with IITs and CSIR are piloting low-water steelmaking and alternative cooling technologies. These efforts support SAIL’s broader goals of sustainability, cost efficiency, and zero-liquid discharge.
• Action plan for reducing CO2 emission: SAIL is pursuing an aggressive decarbonization strategy to reduce CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants. Key technological upgrades include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at improving energy efficiency and cutting fossil fuel use. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being deployed to optimize rolling mill operations. On the renewable front, SAIL is expanding RE to 384 MW by 2028–29, transitioning to LED lighting, and piloting green hydrogen for steelmaking. R&D efforts focus on Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS), low-carbon technologies in collaboration with IITs, and slag utilization to reduce indirect emissions. These initiatives are backed by a phased decarbonization roadmap, active participation in the PAT scheme, and alignment with the National Green Hydrogen Mission—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to sustainable steel production and India’s climate goals.
• Action plan for reducing energy consumption: SAIL is actively working to reduce SEC through a multi-pronged strategy focused on technology, optimization, and innovation. Key process improvements include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at minimizing energy loss and maximizing reuse. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being adopted for their superior efficiency in rolling mills. Energy recycling is enhanced through gas recovery systems and real-time Energy Management Systems (EMS), while load balancing helps reduce peak demand. SAIL is also integrating renewable energy, targeting 384 MW of capacity by 2028–29, and deploying LED lighting with smart controls to cut auxiliary consumption. R&D collaborations with NEDO, IITs, and CSIR are driving low-energy steelmaking innovations, including process integration modeling. These efforts are supported by policy initiatives such as participation in the PAT scheme, alignment with the NMEEE, and regular corporate energy audits—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.
• To enhance its security posture, SAIL is in the process of centralizing its cybersecurity operations to ensure more effective management and defense against emerging threats.

NameOfTheNationalAndInternationalCodesOr.value: SAIL’s manufacturing facilities have well-defined Environment, Health and Safety (EHS) and quality management systems in place and are aligned with International Standards like ISO 14001 for EMS, ISO 9001 for Quality Management Systems, OHSAS 45001 for Occupational Health Safety, ISO 27001 for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS), ISO 50001 for Energy Management System (EnMS), SA 8000 for Social Accountability and ISO 37001 for Anti Bribery Management Systems.

These efforts are supported by policy initiatives such as participation in the PAT scheme, alignment with the NMEEE, and regular corporate energy audits—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.
• To enhance its security posture, SAIL is in the process of centralizing its cybersecurity operations to ensure more effective management and defense against emerging threats.

• SAIL is committed to reducing CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants as part of its sustainability strategy and alignment with India’s climate goals. SAIL aims to lower specific CO2 emissions from 2.60 T/tcs in 2015–16 to below 2.302 T/tcs by 2030, with an interim target of 2.41 T/tcs by 2025–26. SAIL is also working toward achieving net-zero emissions by 2070, in line with national commitments.

SAIL targets a 9% reduction from the 2015–16 baseline of 6.51 Gcal/tcs, aiming to reach 6.08 Gcal/tcs by 2025–26.
• Strengthen enterprise-wide cybersecurity posture through procurement of advanced tools for enhanced incident detection and response capabilities.

PerformanceOfTheEntityAgainstTheSpecific.value:
Details of performance of the entity against the specific commitments, goals and targets for 24-25 is given under the Principle 6.
• Action plan for PM reduction: SAIL is implementing a comprehensive strategy to reduce particulate matter (PM) emissions across its integrated steel plants. This includes upgrading pollution control systems by installing advanced electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), bag filters, wet scrubbers, and dry fog systems. Process optimization measures such as Top Pressure Recovery Turbines (TRT), Coal Dust Injection (CDI), and Coke Dry Quenching (CDQ) are being adopted to minimize emissions. Infrastructure modernization involves commissioning taller coke oven batteries with CDQ and waste heat recovery, along with cleaner reheating furnaces. Material handling is being improved by enclosing conveyors and automating operations to reduce dust. Real-time monitoring is ensured through Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) integrated with a centralized MIS for compliance and audits. SAIL is also focusing on 100% utilization of blast furnace and BOF slag in construction applications to reduce dust from stockpiles. Green belt development is being expanded with native species to enhance dust capture and biodiversity. Employee engagement is promoted through regular environmental training and sustainability initiatives. Additionally, SAIL has partnered with BHP to explore innovative, low-emission steelmaking technologies using hydrogen and biochar, supporting long-term decarbonization and PM reduction goals.
• Action plan for reduction water consumption: SAIL is advancing a robust water conservation strategy to reduce specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. The approach centers on maximizing water recycling through closed-loop systems, reverse osmosis, and membrane bioreactors, along with cascading water use across processes. Traditional cooling systems are being replaced with evaporative towers and air-cooled exchangers, while dry quenching and high-efficiency descaling are cutting water use at the source. Smart sensors, flow meters, and regular audits ensure real-time monitoring and rapid leak detection. Rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge structures are being scaled across facilities. Employee awareness programs promote conservation culture, while R&D collaborations with IITs and CSIR are piloting low-water steelmaking and alternative cooling technologies. These efforts support SAIL’s broader goals of sustainability, cost efficiency, and zero-liquid discharge.
• Action plan for reducing CO2 emission: SAIL is pursuing an aggressive decarbonization strategy to reduce CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants. Key technological upgrades include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at improving energy efficiency and cutting fossil fuel use. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being deployed to optimize rolling mill operations. On the renewable front, SAIL is expanding RE to 384 MW by 2028–29, transitioning to LED lighting, and piloting green hydrogen for steelmaking. R&D efforts focus on Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS), low-carbon technologies in collaboration with IITs, and slag utilization to reduce indirect emissions. These initiatives are backed by a phased decarbonization roadmap, active participation in the PAT scheme, and alignment with the National Green Hydrogen Mission—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to sustainable steel production and India’s climate goals.
• Action plan for reducing energy consumption: SAIL is actively working to reduce SEC through a multi-pronged strategy focused on technology, optimization, and innovation. Key process improvements include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at minimizing energy loss and maximizing reuse. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being adopted for their superior efficiency in rolling mills. Energy recycling is enhanced through gas recovery systems and real-time Energy Management Systems (EMS), while load balancing helps reduce peak demand. SAIL is also integrating renewable energy, targeting 384 MW of capacity by 2028–29, and deploying LED lighting with smart controls to cut auxiliary consumption. R&D collaborations with NEDO, IITs, and CSIR are driving low-energy steelmaking innovations, including process integration modeling. These efforts are supported by policy initiatives such as participation in the PAT scheme, alignment with the NMEEE, and regular corporate energy audits—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.
• To enhance its security posture, SAIL is in the process of centralizing its cybersecurity operations to ensure more effective management and defense against emerging threats.

Material handling is being improved by enclosing conveyors and automating operations to reduce dust. Real-time monitoring is ensured through Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) integrated with a centralized MIS for compliance and audits. SAIL is also focusing on 100% utilization of blast furnace and BOF slag in construction applications to reduce dust from stockpiles. Green belt development is being expanded with native species to enhance dust capture and biodiversity. Employee engagement is promoted through regular environmental training and sustainability initiatives. Additionally, SAIL has partnered with BHP to explore innovative, low-emission steelmaking technologies using hydrogen and biochar, supporting long-term decarbonization and PM reduction goals.
• Action plan for reduction water consumption: SAIL is advancing a robust water conservation strategy to reduce specific water consumption in its integrated steel plants. The approach centers on maximizing water recycling through closed-loop systems, reverse osmosis, and membrane bioreactors, along with cascading water use across processes. Traditional cooling systems are being replaced with evaporative towers and air-cooled exchangers, while dry quenching and high-efficiency descaling are cutting water use at the source. Smart sensors, flow meters, and regular audits ensure real-time monitoring and rapid leak detection. Rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge structures are being scaled across facilities. Employee awareness programs promote conservation culture, while R&D collaborations with IITs and CSIR are piloting low-water steelmaking and alternative cooling technologies. These efforts support SAIL’s broader goals of sustainability, cost efficiency, and zero-liquid discharge.
• Action plan for reducing CO2 emission: SAIL is pursuing an aggressive decarbonization strategy to reduce CO2 emissions in its integrated steel plants. Key technological upgrades include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at improving energy efficiency and cutting fossil fuel use. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being deployed to optimize rolling mill operations. On the renewable front, SAIL is expanding RE to 384 MW by 2028–29, transitioning to LED lighting, and piloting green hydrogen for steelmaking. R&D efforts focus on Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS), low-carbon technologies in collaboration with IITs, and slag utilization to reduce indirect emissions. These initiatives are backed by a phased decarbonization roadmap, active participation in the PAT scheme, and alignment with the National Green Hydrogen Mission—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to sustainable steel production and India’s climate goals.
• Action plan for reducing energy consumption: SAIL is actively working to reduce SEC through a multi-pronged strategy focused on technology, optimization, and innovation. Key process improvements include CDQ, TRT, CDI, WHR systems, all aimed at minimizing energy loss and maximizing reuse. Walking beam reheating furnaces are being adopted for their superior efficiency in rolling mills. Energy recycling is enhanced through gas recovery systems and real-time Energy Management Systems (EMS), while load balancing helps reduce peak demand. SAIL is also integrating renewable energy, targeting 384 MW of capacity by 2028–29, and deploying LED lighting with smart controls to cut auxiliary consumption. R&D collaborations with NEDO, IITs, and CSIR are driving low-energy steelmaking innovations, including process integration modeling. These efforts are supported by policy initiatives such as participation in the PAT scheme, alignment with the NMEEE, and regular corporate energy audits—reinforcing SAIL’s commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.
• To enhance its security posture, SAIL is in the process of centralizing its cybersecurity operations to ensure more effective management and defense against emerging threats.

Schooling, Meals, Uniforms, Textbooks, Accommodation and infrastructure, etc. at Saranda Suvan Chhatravas and Central School, Kiriburu; Gyanodaya Chhatravas, BSP School Rajhara, Bhilai; Gyanjyoti Yojna, Bokaro DAV schools-Nandini & Kuteshwar Mines, and other schools

CSRProject.value: Gyan Jyoti Yojana: Bokaro Steel Plant is providing education and facilitating holistic development for the 15 children of Birhor tribe, which is at the verge of extinction. Children were facilitated with free Education along with boarding, healthy meals, clothing, medical treatment, sports and cultural opportunities in a conducive atmosphere.  They are the first Matriculates and 12th pass from their community. For Skill Development and better employability, 9 Matriculate Birhor Boys  adopted under Gyan Jyoti Yojana had been sponsored for ITI training in ”Welder trade” alongwith stipend, accommodation and meals at Bokaro Pvt ITI.

CSRProject.value: ITI trainings: ITI trainings have been supported for youths in peripherals at ITCs Bolani, Bargaon, Baliapur, Bokaro Pvt ITI and Rourkela etc. The ITIs at Bolani and Bursua have been adopted for upgradation and operation by SAIL/RSP mines. Also, at Bokaro Pvt. ITI youths from the periphery are being trained in streams of Electrician, Welder and Fitter.
